summary:
NASA launched the Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 spacecraft in 1977 to take advantage of a rare planetary alignment that would allow a single trajectory to visit multiple outer planets using gravitational assist maneuvers. Voyager 2 launched first, on August 20, 1977, followed by Voyager 1 on September 5. Despite its later launch, Voyager 1 flew a faster, shorter path and reached Jupiter first in March 1979.

The twin probes returned the first close-up images and detailed scientific data from Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune, transforming humanity's understanding of the outer solar system. Among the most significant discoveries were active volcanoes on Jupiter's moon Io, evidence of a subsurface ocean beneath Europa's ice, and complex weather systems including Neptune's Great Dark Spot. Both spacecraft carried the Golden Record, a phonograph disk containing sounds and images intended to communicate human civilization to any extraterrestrial intelligence that might encounter the probes. By century's end, both Voyagers were still transmitting from the outermost reaches of the solar system, journeying toward interstellar space.
